But it’s a nonbinding resolution, meaning it doesn’t prevent a president from pardoning him in the future.

tweets.text: Tbh I’ve had my skepticism re network school in the past, largely because many of its online evangelists sound like cultists. I’ve read Balajis work cover to cover and find him to be one of the few thinkers, and perhaps the leading thinker which conceptualises cryptocurrencies and blockchain tech as a social technology. Ive criticised NS in the past (again mainly for the cringe evangelists) but having met Balaji in person and had the privilege to discuss an “opt in” internet based social contract, I can conclude he absolutely is a visionary. But what impressed me most was not his depth of thought (we had a 2-3 hour long debate on the social contract and nation state concepts), but that he puts his money where his mouth is. Throughout that 3 hours, Balaji showed himself to not just be a guy in an ivory tower but a practical nation builder. He gave off the vibes of a mayor in a frontier town as we walked the grounds together, discussing immigration, construction, fucking electricity and power down to minute details. He lives there 5 days a week in the same accommodation he sells to people living there. And while perhaps for marketing reasons, it seems that NS shies away from the Malaysian ghost town reality, Balaji was pretty upfront that Forest City was a failed Chinese ghost town that he wanted to populate from the cloud. Honestly walked away from it having very deep respect and admiration for the guy. Him winning here would be crypto, at its very fundamental levels, winning. Will gladly support anything @ns does.
tweets.summary: Tbh I’ve had my skepticism re network school in the past, largely because many of its online evangelists sound like cultists. I’ve read Balajis work…

tweets.text: Ofcom's announcements around its new investigation into TikTok this morning are unusually media-forward. Normally, when the agency opens a new enforcement file, it is not quite this noisy. In early 2025, Ofcom opened with letters. In several high-profile attempted enforcements against my clients, you would also see embargoed press releases or public comment made to fairly low-circulation trade press. Doing a media blitz with agency personnel on national broadcast media (BBC Breakfast/LBC) is, in terms of the agency's normal pattern, highly unusual. My suspicion is that Ofcom is front-loading a PR campaign to manage public perception of its role, where prior enforcement actions have generally not resulted in the desired outcome by no fault of Ofcom's, but rather due to defects in the OSA's design by Parliament. The OSA's extraterritorial enforcement failures (many fines issued, only one collected from a domestic target) are, by now, well-known. Making a big story out of another, new, domestic UK target - TikTok, having London offices, is reachable, not judgment-proof, and compliance is likely - is making a story out of an enforcement case that Ofcom can actually win, without explaining the structural reason for that. The reason is that domestic enforcement cases are the only winnable ones, because a UK penalty is only as good as the UK's ability to collect it (and doing so beyond its borders is challenging if not impossible).
tweets.summary: Ofcom's announcements around its new investigation into TikTok this morning are unusually media-forward. Normally, when the agency opens a new…

tweets.text: On this day we remember Sergeant Micheal C. Chesna, a six year veteran of the Weymouth Police Department who was shot and killed after responding to reports of an erratic operator near South Shore Hospital. Upon arrival, he observed the vehicle had crashed and the driver was vandalizing a home nearby. Sergeant Chesna was struck in the head with a large rock, disarmed and fatally shot by the suspect, who also fatally shot Vera Adams in her home. The suspect was subsequently arrested by responding officers and taken into custody. The suspect has since been convicted of first-degree murder in the death of Sergeant Chesna and second-degree murder in the death of Vera Adams. Sergeant Chesna was a U.S. Army Veteran and recipient of the Purple Heart. He is survived by his wife and two children. Sergeant Michael C. Chesna eight years have passed, you are not forgotten.

tweets.text: RT @kakashiii111: The global electronics component shortage of 2000 remains a legendary supply chain case study. It occurred at the absolute peak of the dot-com bubble, a unique moment when multiple major technological shifts converged simultaneously. The market was hit by the explosive growth of mobile phones, the rapid build-out of internet routing infrastructure, a booming personal computer market, and a massive wave of corporate hardware upgrades driven by pre-millennium Y2K fears. When global manufacturing capacity couldn't keep pace, essential components such as flash memory quickly ran short, creating the perfect conditions for memory manufacturers to organize a cartel and discuss pricing and allocation. The dot-com era was not the first time this happened. The fact that only a handful of companies controlled the global memory market created fertile ground for price matching and allocation coordination. And that brings us to today. The 2000 case study can help us better understand today's market dynamics, supply chain developments, and the forces shaping the memory industry. Read my article: https://t.co/FuIgjpNHmj

tweets.text: RT @adam_minehardt: CLARITY is stalled exactly where the arithmetic puts it, and attention was never the problem. 5 of 8 steps are done: House passed it 294-134, Senate Banking advanced it 15-9, calendar-eligible since June 1. The last three (60 votes in Senate, House passage, POTUS signature) are the hard part, with no cloture motion filed and about three usable Senate weeks before the August recess. Three disputes are doing the blocking: ethics/insider-trading, Section 604 developer liability, stablecoin yield. And unlike GENIUS, CLARITY has no bank-and-payments constituency forcing them shut. The real risk is inertia. Polymarket has been the live whip count: ~59% in June, sub-40% after the July 4 miss, ~43% now. It's tracking the same disputes staffers are negotiating. This isn't dead, and it's nowhere near guaranteed. The next three weeks are the closing act.
tweets.summary: RT @adam_minehardt: CLARITY is stalled exactly where the arithmetic puts it, and attention was never the problem. 5 of 8 steps are done: House passed…

tweets.text: The founders who win won't be the ones refusing to build anything a normal person could use, and they're also not the ones simply reproducing TradFi on crypto rails. Decentralization and permissionless were never about grandma managing her own LTV ratios and praying that the oracle doesn't get manipulated at 2am on a Saturday. It was about ensuring that anyone can get a transaction included without permission, hold and exit their assets without an intermediary's consent, verify the ledger's state without having to trust some party to report it, and build on top without asking anyone's permission. If things get built on top that are gated, that's not inconsistent with decentralization - it's just what technology and infrastructure looks like when it matures.

tweets.text: WATCH: @USRepMikeFlood on reining in the CFPB's regulatory overreach: "I had a lot of disagreements with your predecessor, Director Chopra. Among them was Director Chopra's tendency to expand the CFPB's jurisdictional reach at every turn. For example, the CFPB issued a rulemaking in late 2024 defining digital consumer payment technology entities as 'larger participants' and, therefore, subject to CFPB supervision.
